source.title: Peripheral Nerve Entrapment and Injury in the Upper Extremity

signals: detailed history and physical examination alone are often enough to identify the injury
advanced diagnostic testing with magnetic resonance imaging, ultrasonography, or electrodiagnostic studies can help confirm the clinical diagnosis
nerve injury is divided into three grades of increasing severity: neurapraxia, axonotmesis, and neurotmesis
patients with nerve injury typically present with pain, weakness, and paresthesia

extraction.summary: This guideline covers peripheral nerve entrapment and injury in the upper extremity, focusing on diagnosis through detailed history and physical examination. The primary diagnostic approach involves recognizing patterns of pain, weakness, and paresthesia based on anatomical nerve distributions and specific physical examination findings.

population.risk_criteria: Contact sports participation, overhead activities, repetitive use, anatomic abnormalities, trauma history
population.disease_definition: Peripheral nerve injury classified into three grades: neurapraxia (mild ischemia from compression), axonotmesis (axon damage with preserved connective tissue), and neurotmesis (full-thickness nerve damage)
population.exclusions: Systemic disease, muscle injury as primary cause of weakness/paresthesia
population.severity_staging: Neurapraxia (complete recovery in days to weeks), Axonotmesis (recovery takes years, often incomplete), Neurotmesis (full clinical recovery usually not achieved)

key_thresholds.context: Before considering surgery for most nerve injuries

key_thresholds.parameter: Conservative treatment duration for nerve transection
key_thresholds.context: Long thoracic and spinal accessory nerve injuries

key_thresholds.parameter: Median nerve cross-sectional area at carpal tunnel
key_thresholds.context: Threshold for carpal tunnel syndrome diagnosis on ultrasonography (87.3% sensitivity, 83.3% specificity)
Imaging can be initiated after this period unless severe weakness or multiple nerve involvement

key_thresholds.parameter: Conservative treatment duration before surgery consideration
key_thresholds.context: No improvement after this period indicates surgical consideration

recommendations.recommendation: History and physical examination alone are often enough to identify nerve injury or entrapment
recommendations.notes: Focus on trauma history, time course, aggravating activities, symptom distribution

recommendations.recommendation: Advanced diagnostic testing with MRI, ultrasonography, or electrodiagnostic studies should be used if conservative management is ineffective

recommendations.notes: Surgical options available for refractory injuries or anatomic abnormality

recommendations.recommendation: Return to play acceptable after first stinger episode when complete symptom resolution and cervical spine injury excluded
recommendations.notes: Persistent or recurrent stingers require additional evaluation

recommendations.recommendation: Nocturnal splinting more effective than placebo for carpal tunnel syndrome
recommendations.notes: Maximal benefit typically achieved around 3 months

recommendations.recommendation: Use physical examination findings in combination with electrodiagnostic studies to increase diagnostic accuracy before surgical intervention
recommendations.evidence_level: Expert opinion and clinical practice guideline
recommendations.notes: Especially important for carpal tunnel syndrome

recommendations.recommendation: Electrodiagnostic testing should be used as adjunct to physical exam and imaging
recommendations.notes: Helps confirm diagnosis, establish severity, and monitor progression

recommendations.recommendation: Initial treatment should be conservative in absence of traumatic injury
recommendations.notes: Include patient education, physical therapy, and activity modification

recommendations.recommendation: Use ultrasonography and MRI for diagnosing anatomic causes of nerve entrapment
recommendations.notes: Particularly useful for identifying space-occupying lesions

recommendations.recommendation: Use Phalen test and Hoffmann-Tinel sign for carpal tunnel syndrome diagnosis
recommendations.notes: Physical examination maneuvers for clinical diagnosis

recommendations.recommendation: Perform electrodiagnostic testing for carpal tunnel syndrome confirmation
recommendations.notes: Based on American Association of Electrodiagnostic Medicine guidelines

recommendations.recommendation: Consider ultrasonography for diagnosing carpal tunnel syndrome
Use proximal radial motor conduction testing for acute compressive radial neuropathy
recommendations.notes: Useful diagnostic tool for radial nerve compression

medications.indication: Pain management for various nerve entrapments
Therapeutic and diagnostic for multiple nerve entrapments

red_flags: Evidence of moderate to severe median nerve damage on electromyography
Significant weakness of grip strength with thenar eminence atrophy
Fracture of the humerus resulting in nerve compromise
Penetrating trauma resulting in nerve transection
Evidence of anatomic abnormalities predisposing to repeat injury
extraction.follow_up: Serial examination with electromyography and nerve conduction studies for monitoring recovery. Physical therapy recovery monitoring at 6 months for radial neuropathy.
extraction.escalation_path: Refer for surgery if: no electrophysiologic improvement after 3-4 months of conservative treatment for most nerve injuries, evidence of anatomic abnormalities predisposing to repeat injury, space-occupying lesions, or no improvement after 18-24 months for long thoracic/spinal accessory nerve injuries. Early surgery indicated for moderate to severe median nerve damage on electromyography.
